message.content: Actually let's do the `vc-x1 push ...` so we push code & bot repo and let ti update ochid's.

input.command: vc-x1 push --help 2>&1

content.content:
vc-x1 0.62.0-7

Dual-repo commit+push+finalize in one resumable command.

Collapses today's manual Commit-Push-Finalize Flow into a
single subcommand with two interactive approval gates and a
state machine with persistent progress so interruptions can
resume without re-doing completed stages.

Stages: preflight (fmt/clippy/test) → review (approve diff)
→ message ($EDITOR / --title+--body, approve text) →
commit-app → commit-claude (skipped if clean) → bookmark-both
→ push-app → finalize-claude. Failures in commit-app /
commit-claude / bookmark-both roll both repos back via
`jj op restore` to the snapshot recorded before commit-app.
After push-app succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and
recovery is forward-only.

Non-interactive use: pass both --title and --body plus --yes
to skip the review gate. Saved state carries title/body
across resumes so only the first invocation needs them.

Usage: vc-x1 push [OPTIONS] [BOOKMARK]

Arguments:
  [BOOKMARK]
          Bookmark to advance in both repos (positional form of `--bookmark`).
          
          Accepting a positional lets the common case read as `vc-x1 push main`
          without the `--bookmark` ceremony; `--bookmark` is kept as an alias
          for scripts and for composition with other args. The two forms
          conflict if both supplied.

Options:
      --bookmark <BOOKMARK>
          Bookmark to advance in both repos (flag form; see positional)

  -V, --version
          Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then continue. With
          no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.
          
          Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after printing): the
          banner now rides along with normal subcommand execution rather than
          gating it, so scripts can capture the version *and* the command's
          output in one invocation.

      --restart
          Clear any saved state file and start from stage 1

  -v, --verbose...
          Verbose output: -v debug, -vv trace

      --from <STAGE>
          Explicit stage to jump to (advanced / debug use)

          Possible values:
          - preflight:       Run fmt / clippy / test / install / retest
          - review:          Present diff for the first approval gate
          - message:         Compose / edit the commit message; present for
            second gate
          - commit-app:      Commit the app repo
          - commit-claude:   Commit the `.claude` session repo (skipped if
            empty)
          - bookmark-both:   Advance both bookmarks to `@-`
          - push-app:        `jj git push --bookmark <b> -R .`
          - finalize-claude: `vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <b>
            ...`

      --log <LOG>
          Log file path (captures all levels)

      --step
          Pause between every stage for an interactive approval gate

      --status
          Print where the saved state thinks we are and exit

      --recheck
          Re-run `preflight` even on resume (default: skip if last run
          succeeded)

      --no-finalize
          Stop before `finalize-claude` so it can be run manually

      --dry-run
          Print the exact commands for every stage without side effects

      --title <STR>
          Commit title (skip `$EDITOR` for the message stage)

      --body <STR>
          Commit body (skip `$EDITOR` for the message stage)

  -y, --yes
          Auto-approve interactive prompts (review gate, message-edit
          confirmation). Required when `--title` / `--body` aren't both supplied
          in non-interactive contexts

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

toolUseResult.stdout:
vc-x1 0.62.0-7

Dual-repo commit+push+finalize in one resumable command.

Collapses today's manual Commit-Push-Finalize Flow into a
single subcommand with two interactive approval gates and a
state machine with persistent progress so interruptions can
resume without re-doing completed stages.

Stages: preflight (fmt/clippy/test) → review (approve diff)
→ message ($EDITOR / --title+--body, approve text) →
commit-app → commit-claude (skipped if clean) → bookmark-both
→ push-app → finalize-claude. Failures in commit-app /
commit-claude / bookmark-both roll both repos back via
`jj op restore` to the snapshot recorded before commit-app.
After push-app succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and
recovery is forward-only.

Non-interactive use: pass both --title and --body plus --yes
to skip the review gate. Saved state carries title/body
across resumes so only the first invocation needs them.

Usage: vc-x1 push [OPTIONS] [BOOKMARK]

Arguments:
  [BOOKMARK]
          Bookmark to advance in both repos (positional form of `--bookmark`).
          
          Accepting a positional lets the common case read as `vc-x1 push main`
          without the `--bookmark` ceremony; `--bookmark` is kept as an alias
          for scripts and for composition with other args. The two forms
          conflict if both supplied.

Options:
      --bookmark <BOOKMARK>
          Bookmark to advance in both repos (flag form; see positional)

  -V, --version
          Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then continue. With
          no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.
          
          Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after printing): the
          banner now rides along with normal subcommand execution rather than
          gating it, so scripts can capture the version *and* the command's
          output in one invocation.

      --restart
          Clear any saved state file and start from stage 1

  -v, --verbose...
          Verbose output: -v debug, -vv trace

      --from <STAGE>
          Explicit stage to jump to (advanced / debug use)

          Possible values:
          - preflight:       Run fmt / clippy / test / install / retest
          - review:          Present diff for the first approval gate
          - message:         Compose / edit the commit message; present for
            second gate
          - commit-app:      Commit the app repo
          - commit-claude:   Commit the `.claude` session repo (skipped if
            empty)
          - bookmark-both:   Advance both bookmarks to `@-`
          - push-app:        `jj git push --bookmark <b> -R .`
          - finalize-claude: `vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <b>
            ...`

      --log <LOG>
          Log file path (captures all levels)

      --step
          Pause between every stage for an interactive approval gate

      --status
          Print where the saved state thinks we are and exit

      --recheck
          Re-run `preflight` even on resume (default: skip if last run
          succeeded)

      --no-finalize
          Stop before `finalize-claude` so it can be run manually

      --dry-run
          Print the exact commands for every stage without side effects

      --title <STR>
          Commit title (skip `$EDITOR` for the message stage)

      --body <STR>
          Commit body (skip `$EDITOR` for the message stage)

  -y, --yes
          Auto-approve interactive prompts (review gate, message-edit

file.content:
### Gap list (refreshed for 0.62.0+)

The original pre-design gaps are now resolved by the
negotiated decisions (Decisions blocks under each axis).
This list captures the **implementation-cycle gaps** the
design surfaces — concrete work for 0.62.0+ Todo entries.
Ordered (roughly) from cheapest equalization (smallest
blast radius) to largest, consistent with the
`## Commonality` ranking.

1. **`validate-desc` / `fix-desc` equalization** — local
   refactor: replace the `other_repo_from_config` prelude
   with a scope-aware resolution that no-ops when
   `Side::Bot` is absent. Body unchanged. Cheapest concrete
   equalization and the right prototype for the
   topology-from-config rule.
2. **`CommonArgs`-family por test coverage** — pure test
   work. Add `FixturePor` exercises to `chid` / `desc` /
   `show` / `list` tests. Surfaces bugs without
   architectural risk.
3. **`--mode=<single|dual>` flag** — replace today's
   boolean `--por` with one value-bearing flag (`single`
   ≡ por, default `dual`, optional `s` / `d` aliases).
   Threads through `init` / `clone` + becomes runtime override
   on every other subcommand.
4. **`--public` peer flag** — add `--public` as peer to
   `--private`. Exactly-one-of. Needed so users with
   `[default].private = true` in config can spell
   "public" per invocation.
5. **`--repo none` category** — third peer to `remote` /
   `local`. `init` / `clone` skip remote provisioning;
   `--account` skipped when `--repo none` resolves.
6. **Workspace `.vc-config.toml` write dropped under por**
   — today's degenerate `[workspace] path = "/"` write
   removed. Por never reads, never creates the workspace
   metadata file.
7. **`default_scope` broken-dual detection** — today's
   silent fallback to `Scope([Code])` when `.vc-config.toml`
   is missing in a `.claude/`-present workspace is a
   footgun. Add explicit broken-dual error. Prerequisite

means broken-dual detection has to exist first.

#7 before #9) override size ordering." Doesn't require
renumbering.

# Max review #1 — 0.61.0 por/dual parity design

Captured 2026-05-26 (max effort) after the close-out commit
of 0.61.0 (`c0f3376f`, pre-push to `main`). Independent
review of the design output (audit doc, copying stub,
chores narrative). Each item below carries a **Status** —
open (needs decision), resolved (with the agreed
disposition), or applied (already changed).

## Scope reviewed

- [`por-dual-parity-audit.md`](por-dual-parity-audit.md) —
  canonical design doc post-0.61.0 (≈1090 lines).
- [`copying.md`](copying.md) — copying mechanism design
  stub from `0.61.0-4`.
- [`por-dual-parity.md`](por-dual-parity.md) — older
  forward-looking doc (read for context only; not
  critiqued).
- [`/notes/chores/chores-12.md > ## docs: por/dual parity
  design (0.61.0)`](../chores/chores-12.md#docs-pordual-parity-design-0610) —
  cycle narrative.

Line numbers in this review are **as of the 0.61.0
close-out snapshot**; section / heading names are the
durable references.

## Strengths

For balance — what the bot thinks works well:

- **Three-pass methodology** (audit → commonality
  inversion → axis decomposition). The `-2` inversion is
  the cycle's pivotal move: reframed "we have a problem
  everywhere" to "the right pattern already exists, extend
  it." That mental flip made the rest tractable.
- **Axis decomposition is genuine decomposition.** Calling
  out `--por` as a bundle of six axes opens design space
  the bundle hid. The litmus is `audit.md:752` — "A user
  wanting a fully-plain single repo today can't spell it" —
  a real defect of the bundle framing.
- **A2 collapse was earned.** `.vc-config.toml` write was
  initially listed as independent; the design pass
  noticed presence-of-`.vc-config.toml` ≡ Topology and
  retired the axis. Honest housekeeping.
- **Two-class principle ("defaults where natural; errors
  only for user-specific keys") is sound.** Correct
  generalization of what `resolve_repo` already does.
  Baked-in default config + `vc-x1 config dump` closes
  the "what does layer-4-empty mean?" question
  elegantly.
- **T7-only promotion.** Resisting the urge to seed all
  14 Gaps into Todo is the right restraint — cheapest
  concrete equalization that validates the
  topology-from-config rule as the prototype.
- **Status note + "implementation will diverge" framing**
  (`audit.md:19-37`) is humility the bot will thank
  itself for in 0.65.0.

## Concerns

### 6. Gap-list ordering hides one structural dependency

**Critique:** `audit.md:1010-1080` ranks gaps by "blast
radius." But Gap #7 (`default_scope` broken-dual
detection) is a *prerequisite* for Gap #9 (copying
mechanism), per `copying.md:81-92` — copying defers
validation to the first downstream subcommand, which
means broken-dual detection has to exist first.

**Proposed action:** one-line note above the list:
"ranking is rough; some structural prerequisites (e.g.
#7 before #9) override size ordering." Doesn't require
renumbering.

**Status:** open. Docs-only edit.

## Nits

Lower-priority items; do as time allows.

- **`finalize` matrix row** marks T+SC support based on
  the body being topology-neutral. True, but no command
  path exercises it today. The bot thinks adding a
  footnote — "currently latent — body supports it, no
  caller surfaces it" — would prevent a future cycle
  hardening a use case nobody asked for.

- **Reading guide at top of audit doc.** The doc is ≈1090
  lines and will be cited from many cycles. A `## Reading
  guide` (matrix → §X; one axis → §Y; new axis → §Z)
  costs 10 lines and pays back across many readings. The
  bot thinks this is worth doing before 0.62.0 starts.

- **"Por's view of the chain"** (`audit.md:897-913`)
  duplicates the per-axis Decisions blocks. The bot
  thinks it could be dropped or reframed as a
  debugging cheat-sheet ("when does topology actually
  get consulted?") — a different angle.

- **`validate-todo` / `fix-todo`** are topology-blind by
  design. Worth one sentence somewhere ("notes/-family
  commands operate outside the workspace shape") so a
  future reader doesn't suspect they were missed.

## Process observation

The user-proposed-then-deferred `~/.config/vc-x1/config.
toml` topology default that **opened** the cycle is, by
close-out, **in** the design (`audit.md:763`:
`[default].topology = "single" | "dual"`). The bot's
initial "audit first, expose defaults later" pushback
was about *ordering*, not the idea itself. Net: the cycle
delivered what the user originally proposed, just through
a more thorough process.

The bot thinks this is worth noting because it shows the
pushback shape ("don't ship that *yet*") working
correctly — but also that the original ask was the right
ask. The Decisions blocks currently read as if the design
emerged from the bot's analysis alone; an acknowledgment
of the original user framing would balance the record.

**Status:** open. Optional chores narrative tweak (no
action implied for the design itself).

## Disposition table

| # | Title | Status | Action surface |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 6 | Gap-list ordering hides one prereq | Open | `audit.md` Gap list intro |
| N1 | `finalize` matrix row footnote | Open | `audit.md` matrix |
| N2 | Reading guide at top of audit doc | Open | `audit.md` top |
| N3 | "Por's view of the chain" redundancy | Open | `audit.md` § "Por's view" |
| N4 | `validate-todo` / `fix-todo` topology-blind note | Open | `audit.md` (anywhere appropriate) |
| Pr | Original user framing acknowledgment | Open | Either `chores-12.md` cycle narrative or `audit.md` Decisions blocks |

## TL;DR

The two highest-conviction concerns — 1 (runtime `--por`
semantics) and 3 (copying-surface doubling) — are applied
and removed from this list. What remains (concern #6,
nits N1–N4, and the process observation) is docs-only: low
risk to defer, but cheaper to apply now in one design pass
than after the next ten cycles cite the current text.

# References

[1]: /notes/design-cli/por-dual-parity-audit.md
[2]: /notes/design-cli/copying.md
[3]: /notes/design-cli/por-dual-parity.md
[4]: /notes/chores/chores-12.md#docs-pordual-parity-design-0610

+_All concerns applied and removed from this list; the
+Disposition table below and the git log are the record._
-validation to the first downstream subcommand, which
-means broken-dual detection has to exist first.
-**Proposed action:** one-line note above the list:
-#7 before #9) override size ordering." Doesn't require
Lower-priority items; do as time allows.

content.text:
Work complete for `0.62.0-8` (Concern #6). Please review.

**What changed** (docs-only):
- `notes/design-cli/por-dual-parity-audit.md` — appended a note to the Gap list intro: ranking is rough, structural prerequisites override size ordering, with the concrete `#7 before #9` example (broken-dual detection before copying's deferred validation).
- `notes/design-cli/max-review-1.md`:
  - deleted the applied Concern #6 section. Since it was the last concern, the now-empty `## Concerns` section gets a **drained-marker** line (`_All concerns applied… the Disposition table and git log are the record._`) instead of an empty header.
  - removed its disposition row.
  - TL;DR "what remains" → `nits N1–N4 and the process observation`.
- `Cargo.toml` / `Cargo.lock` — bump to `0.62.0-8`.
- `notes/todo.md` — `-8` flipped to `(done)`.

**One choice to confirm:** the drained-marker. Prior commits just deleted the concern (which left siblings behind), but #6 was the last one — so I left an explicit "all applied" marker rather than an empty `## Concerns` header or a silent removal, matching the project's "don't let it look missed" instinct (cf. nit N4). Happy to instead drop the `## Concerns` header entirely, or keep it bare — your call.

The cargo cycle + `vc-x1 -V` install will run as part of `vc-x1 push`'s preflight when we push. Ready for the TB whenever you've reviewed.

message.content: LG, should we add that little tib-bit, that there should be a "pre-commit" prior to review, and the one in push is "safety-net"?
LG, should we add that little tib-bit to CLAUDE.md, that there should be a "pre-commit" prior to review, and the one in push is "safety-net"?

content.text:
Good idea — it's worth codifying, but let me pin down exactly *what* and *where*, because there's a subtlety.

The per-commit flow in `cycle-protocol.md` **already** orders the cargo cycle (step 4) before the Work review (step 5) — so "checks before review" is technically already the documented sequence. What's *not* captured is the two things you just articulated:

1. **The cargo cycle is the primary gate; push's `preflight` is a redundant safety-net** — don't lean on push to catch what review should already have seen green.
2. The interaction with **"skip-able for purely-docs commits"** — that escape hatch is what let me skip the cycle on `-7`/`-8` initially. Your principle ("I want to know all is well while reviewing") partly argues against skipping even on docs commits (the value there is the `cargo install` + `vc-x1 -V` confirming the version bump compiles and installs).

So before I draft text, two questions on scope:

questions.question: How should the "skip-able for purely-docs commits" guidance change, given you want checks-before-review?

options.label: Keep skip-able, add safety-net note
options.description: Leave docs commits skip-able, but add that when you DO run the cycle it's the primary gate and push's preflight is just a safety-net. Least invasive.

options.label: Run before review even for docs
options.description: Change guidance: run the cycle (at least cargo install + vc-x1 -V) before review even on docs-only commits, so review sees a known-good installed build. Push preflight is the safety-net.

options.label: Run, but only install+-V for docs
options.description: Full fmt/clippy/test only when code changes; for docs commits require just cargo install + vc-x1 -V before review to confirm the version bump builds/installs.

questions.question: When should this CLAUDE.md / cycle-protocol edit land?

options.label: Capture as Idea, do later
options.description: Add to notes/todo.md ## Ideas now; keep the current 0.62.0 max-review ladder focused. Pick it up in a later cycle.

options.label: Slot into this cycle now
options.description: Insert as a dedicated commit (e.g. 0.62.0-9, before the nits) in the current cycle. It's a docs/process change, on-theme with the docs cycle.

aiTitle: Run pre-commit and verify vc-x1 installation

content.text: What would you like to clarify? I'm happy to reframe the questions or answer anything about the options first.

message.content: As it stands our current pre-commit doesn't check non-code things, but I can see adding validate-todo and to the pre-commit in which case that would be added as an exception. Seems to be we have one rule and no exception for "doc" only changes, my initial reaction. Thoughts?

content.text:
Your instinct is right, and I think the "one rule, no exception" framing is the better target. Here's my reasoning:

**Why the exception exists today** — the pre-commit is *only* the cargo cycle (fmt/clippy/test/install), which checks code. On a docs-only commit there's no code to check, so "skip-able" was a reasonable carve-out. But that exact carve-out is what let me skip checks on `-7`/`-8` until you asked — exceptions are where things slip.

**Why your idea closes the gap** — once the pre-commit also runs `validate-todo` (and, later, a `validate-repo` that checks anchors / chores-header sync — already foreshadowed in CLAUDE.md), there *is* something to validate on a docs commit. At that point "skip-able for docs" stops making sense: the rule becomes "always run pre-commit before review," full stop. One rule, no exception.

So I'd frame it as: **the exception was a symptom of an under-powered pre-commit, not a real property of docs commits.** Fix the pre-commit and the exception dissolves on its own — cleaner than documenting a carve-out.

**One scoping wrinkle** — "the pre-commit" lives in two places:
- the *documented* flow in `cycle-protocol.md` (step 4) + the CLAUDE.md summary — pure docs to change.
- `vc-x1 push`'s `preflight` *stage* (in `push.rs`) — adding `validate-todo` there is a **code** change, and it'd want a test.

So the full idea is one docs edit + one code change. My recommendation for decomposition:

1. **Now / cheap (docs):** adopt the single-rule framing — drop "skip-able for purely-docs," state pre-commit runs before review, and add the "push preflight is a redundant safety-net" note.
2. **Follow-up (code, its own Todo):** add `validate-todo` (then `validate-repo`) to both the documented pre-commit and push's `preflight` stage, with a test.

Doing #1 immediately means docs commits run the cargo cycle that, for now, mainly confirms the version bump builds/installs (`vc-x1 -V`) — thin but not worthless, and it stops the skip. #2 makes the rule genuinely meaningful for docs.

Question of sequencing: do you want #1 folded into *this* `0.62.0` cycle (it's on-theme — docs/process), or captured for a fresh cycle so the max-review ladder stays clean? And is the interim state in #1 (single rule, but pre-commit not yet validating docs) acceptable to you, or would you rather hold the whole thing until #2 lands together?

content.text:
Grounded now: `main@origin` is `0.62.0-7`, and the chain is `0.61.0 → 0.62.0-0 → … → 0.62.0-7` — all eight `0.62.0-x` commits are **pushed**. So here's my honest read of the 0.61.1-insert idea:

**The mechanics work, but the cost isn't conflicts — it's a published-history rewrite.** Inserting `0.61.1` between `0.61.0` and `0.62.0-0` means rebasing all 8 published `0.62.0-x` commits (plus their 8 `.claude` counterparts) onto the new commit, then **force-pushing both repos**. You're right that auto-rebase would sail through — CLAUDE.md is untouched in `0.62.0-x` so there's no textual conflict, and chids are stable so the `ochid:` linkage survives. But conflicts were never the risk; rewriting 16 already-published commits across two repos is, and the protocol explicitly flags remote rewrites as approval-gated, careful operations.

**And `0.61.1` is semantically arbitrary anyway** — the process-rule change isn't a patch to `0.61.0`'s por/dual content; it's independent of both cycles. So slotting it *below* buys you a rewrite *and* an arbitrary version label.

**The goal you're after — its own version slot, max-review ladder left clean — is cheaper to get on top:**

- **`0.62.1` as a fresh patch cycle after `0.62.0` close-out** — correct ordering (`0.62.1 > 0.62.0`), no rewrite, genuinely independent slot. This is the clean version of your instinct.
- **Fold as `0.62.0-9`** — simplest, no rewrite; only cost is the max-review ladder picks up one process commit (mild, and it's still docs).

For a process-doc change, *where it sits in history carries no value* — it just needs to be on `main`. So I'd skip the rewrite entirely and go `0.62.1`-on-top (if you want the clean slot) or `-9` (if you want it done now). My lean: **fold as `-9`** — it's a few lines of CLAUDE.md + cycle-protocol, and a separate cycle is ceremony for a change this small.

Want me to draft the `-9` edit, or set it up as `0.62.1` for after close-out?

message.content:
0.62.1 is cleaner and we'll go that route for now, (add as top of P1). But there is another issue
that comes to mind, our protocol of have version numbers in Titles/Bodies/todo/done/... is wrong.
Version number are subject to change. For instance on a "public" repo, like linux, the versions
numbers are defined only when a change is merged into "main" and we can't anticipate what
version a change we're working on now would land with.

So this protocol is wrong, I'm no idea what we should do but add "version number protocol"
to our Todo::Ideas list.

   the gate rather than a redundant safety-net.

     Work review on every commit; push's `preflight` is a

     exists) to both the documented flow and push's `preflight`
     stage (`push.rs`), with a test. (code)

2. **Version-number protocol is fragile — versions are
   baked into titles/bodies/todo/done/chores before the
   change lands.** The cycle protocol embeds an `X.Y.Z-N`
   version in commit titles and bodies, `## Todo` /
   `## Done` entries, and chores headers — all written
   while the work is in progress, i.e. before it lands.
   But version numbers are subject to change: in a public,
   merge-based flow (e.g. Linux), the version a change
   ships under is only fixed when it merges into `main`,
   so the landing version can't be anticipated while the
   work is underway. Pervasive version-in-text is

## Priorities

- P1 is highest priority, same priority are grouped equally
- A line with `...` is a prefix-match against a `## Todo` title
  - resolves locally (in this file); should be unique
- Entries without a prefix-match are free-form
  - chores tasks, ad-hoc reminders.

### P1

- `**pre-commit: single rule...**`
- `**vc-x1 push: body starting with `-` breaks `commit-app`.**`
- `**vc-x1 push: support...**`
- `**vc-x1 push --squash...**`

### P2

- `**single-field...**`

### P3

- `**por -> dual...**`

## Todo

 Prioritized entries (referenced by `## Priorities`) lower priority
 todo's are in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). In all cases
 we use the [Prose Form in CLAUDE.md](/CLAUDE.md#prose-form). When
 more detail is waranted those reside in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`.
 Also, we use the  design subsections (link via `[N]` ref). Run
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.

1. **vc-x1 push: body starting with `-` breaks `commit-app`.**
   Push builds `jj commit -m "<title>" -m "<body>"` in the
   space form, so a body whose first line is a bullet
   (`- file: …`, the normal app-repo body shape) makes jj's
   parser reject the value as a stray flag. Hit on 0.62.0-5.
   The rollback was clean, but the push can't land a
   standard bullet-first body.
   - Fix: invoke `jj commit -m=<body>` (equals form) or set

input.old_string:
1. **vc-x1 push: body starting with `-` breaks `commit-app`.**
   Push builds `jj commit -m "<title>" -m "<body>"` in the
   space form, so a body whose first line is a bullet
   (`- file: …`, the normal app-repo body shape) makes jj's
   parser reject the value as a stray flag. Hit on 0.62.0-5.
   The rollback was clean, but the push can't land a
   standard bullet-first body.
   - Fix: invoke `jj commit -m=<body>` (equals form) or set
     `allow_hyphen_values` on the message args.
   - The same defect bites a raw `jj commit -m "<body>"`, so
     the equals form is the general remedy.
   - Workaround until fixed: prepend a non-dash intro line
     to the body (a Prose-Form intro sentence).
input.new_string:
1. **vc-x1 push: validate body opens with an intro paragraph.**
   A body whose first line is a bullet (`- file: …`) is a
   Prose-Form violation — bodies must open with an intro
   paragraph, then bullets. Today such a body trips jj's arg
   parser (`jj commit -m "<body>"` reads the leading `-` as a
   stray flag) and push fails with an opaque error. Hit on
   0.62.0-5.
   - Feature, not a parser bug (reframed): push should
     *validate* the body opens with a non-dash intro line and
     flag its absence with a clear, specific error pointing at
     the offending first line — rather than letting jj emit a
     confusing one, or quietly accepting a bullet-first body.
   - Enforcing the intro is the intended behavior, matching
     the Prose-Form convention; we are not "fixing" the parser
     to accept bullet-first bodies.
   - Workaround until the explicit check lands: prepend a
     non-dash intro sentence to the body.

file.content:
## Concerns

_All concerns applied and removed from this list; the
Disposition table below and the git log are the record._

## Nits

Lower-priority items; do as time allows.

- **`finalize` matrix row** marks T+SC support based on
  the body being topology-neutral. True, but no command
  path exercises it today. The bot thinks adding a
  footnote — "currently latent — body supports it, no
  caller surfaces it" — would prevent a future cycle
  hardening a use case nobody asked for.

- **Reading guide at top of audit doc.** The doc is ≈1090
  lines and will be cited from many cycles. A `## Reading
  guide` (matrix → §X; one axis → §Y; new axis → §Z)
  costs 10 lines and pays back across many readings. The
  bot thinks this is worth doing before 0.62.0 starts.

- **"Por's view of the chain"** (`audit.md:897-913`)
  duplicates the per-axis Decisions blocks. The bot
  thinks it could be dropped or reframed as a
  debugging cheat-sheet ("when does topology actually
  get consulted?") — a different angle.

- **`validate-todo` / `fix-todo`** are topology-blind by
  design. Worth one sentence somewhere ("notes/-family
  commands operate outside the workspace shape") so a
  future reader doesn't suspect they were missed.

## Process observation

The user-proposed-then-deferred `~/.config/vc-x1/config.
toml` topology default that **opened** the cycle is, by
close-out, **in** the design (`audit.md:763`:
`[default].topology = "single" | "dual"`). The bot's
initial "audit first, expose defaults later" pushback
was about *ordering*, not the idea itself. Net: the cycle
delivered what the user originally proposed, just through
a more thorough process.

The bot thinks this is worth noting because it shows the
pushback shape ("don't ship that *yet*") working
correctly — but also that the original ask was the right
ask. The Decisions blocks currently read as if the design
emerged from the bot's analysis alone; an acknowledgment
of the original user framing would balance the record.

**Status:** open. Optional chores narrative tweak (no
action implied for the design itself).

## Disposition table

# por/dual parity audit (0.61.0-1)

Read-only snapshot audit of every place the `dual` workspace
topology (code repo + `.claude/` companion, cross-linked by
`ochid:` trailers, configured by `.vc-config.toml`) gets a
privileged code path over `por` (Plain Old Repo — single code
repo, no `.claude/`, no `.vc-config.toml`). Snapshot taken at
commit `463e6bd9` (`docs: prep por/dual parity audit
(0.61.0-0)`). Parent design item is T5 in `todo.md` [[1]];
design stub is `por-dual-parity.md` [[2]].

- Scope: every subcommand, the `--por` flag's reach, the
  ochid-touching helpers, test fixtures.
- Out of scope: proposing equalization shape (each finding
  carries a one-sentence sketch only — concrete fixes are
  follow-up cycles).
- Method: grep + read-through. No code changes.

## Status

**Pre-implementation design snapshot.** This document
captures the audit findings (`## 1`–`## 8`), the
commonality analysis (`## Commonality`), and the
negotiated design (`## Feature axes`) at the end of cycle
0.61.0. The matrix, the per-axis Decisions blocks, and
the resolution chain are the *current target*, not the
final shape.

**The implementation will diverge from this.** Every
design stub does — implementation surfaces edge cases,
forces compromises, and reveals constraints the design
pass didn't anticipate. Treat what's here as the starting
position for 0.62.0+ equalization cycles, not a contract
the code must conform to. When implementation finds the
design wrong, update the design (or annotate the
divergence in chores) — don't bend code to fit a stale
sketch.

## 1. `options_flags/por.rs` — the `--por` gate

#### Por's view of the chain

Following the decisions in this cycle:

- **Workspace `.vc-config.toml`** (layer 3) — dual-only.
  `single` never reads, never creates. Today's degenerate
  `[workspace] path = "/"` write is residue from when the
  flag was a half-implemented boolean opt-out and should
  be dropped.
- **User-config `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml`**
  (layer 4) — topology-neutral. Both single and dual
  consult it.
- **Runtime `--mode=single`** — overrides workspace
  topology detection across every subcommand (not just
  `init`/`clone`). Treats the target repo (`.` by
  default, or `-R/--repo <path>`) as single: no sibling
  traversal, no `[workspace] other-repo` lookup, no
  cross-repo ochid validation. Symmetric across code
  and bot:
  - `--mode=single -R .` (in a dual workspace) → operate
    on code, no sibling.
  - `--mode=single -R .claude` → operate on `.claude`,
    no sibling.
  - `--mode=single -R /vendored/single-repo` → operate
    on that repo as-is.

### Subcommand × parameter matrix

The designed-state at-a-glance view — for each
subcommand (rows), which parameter families it accepts
(columns), and a short note on what the parameter does
in that context.

This is the **post-equalization target**, not today's
state. Today many of these cells are gaps (per the audit
findings); the matrix captures what 0.62.0+ equalization
cycles drive toward.

**Ledger — column acronyms map to axes (or to
cross-cutting flag groups):**

| Acronym | Axis / group | Flags |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **T** | Topology | `--mode=<single\|dual>` (creation on `init`/`clone`; runtime override on every other subcommand) |
| **A/R** | Remote | `--account` + `--repo` (remote provider resolution; user-config-keyed) |
| **Priv** | Privacy | `--visibility=<public\|private>` (GitHub visibility; `init`-only) |
| **CP** | Copying | `--init-from` (file copying into the workspace) |
| **CFG** | *Config paths* (not an axis) | `--config <path>` / `--global-config <path>` (point at non-default config files) |
| **NO-CFG** | *Config skip* (not an axis) | `--no-local-config` / `--no-global-config` (skip layers in the resolution chain) |
| **SC** | *Scope* (runtime selection, not an axis) | `--scope` / `-R` (per-invocation repo selection on dual workspaces) |

The first four acronyms match feature axes (negotiated
in this cycle); the last three are cross-cutting flag
groups that don't correspond to single axes but appear on
many subcommands.

**Cell legend:**

- `✓` — accepted; default behavior.
- `✓*` — accepted with caveat (see footnotes).
- `—` — not applicable for this subcommand.
- `(new)` — subcommand or capability added by the
  0.62.0+ rollout.

| Subcommand | T | A/R | Priv | CP | CFG | NO-CFG | SC |
| --- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| `init <TARGET>` | ✓ creation | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | — |
| `clone <URL>` | ✓ creation | — | — | ✓* | ✓ | ✓ | — |
| `push <BM>` | ✓ runtime | — | — | — | ✓ | ✓ | ✓* |
| `sync` | ✓ runtime | — | — | — | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| `finalize` | ✓ runtime | — | — | — | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| `chid` | ✓ runtime | — | — | — | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| `desc` | ✓ runtime | — | — | — | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| `show` | ✓ runtime | — | — | — | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| `list` | ✓ runtime | — | — | — | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |

1025	
1026	**Footnotes:**
1027	
1028	- `clone CP*` — copying on `clone` overlays *on top of*
1029	  the cloned content (same flag set, same collision
1030	  rules as `init`).
1031	- `clone A/R —, Priv —` — clone resolves its source from
1032	  `<TARGET>` and clones existing repos; it never
1033	  provisions a remote, so the Remote-create and Privacy
1034	  axes don't apply (verified: `clone.rs` has no

1036	- `push SC*` — push uses bookmark scoping rather than

1048	**Topology column (`T`) — quick reference:**
1049	
1050	- *Creation* (init/clone): `--mode=<single|dual>` chooses
1051	  the workspace shape that gets written into
1052	  `.vc-config.toml` (dual only — single writes nothing).
1053	- *Runtime override* (other subcommands): `--mode=single`
1054	  treats the target repo (`.` by default, or
1055	  `-R/--repo <path>`) as single — no sibling
1056	  traversal, no `[workspace] other-repo` lookup.
1057	  Symmetric across code and bot. Default-dual at the
1058	  workspace level still applies (`.vc-config.toml`
1059	  still says dual); `--mode=single` is a per-invocation
1060	  override asking "treat the target as single for
1061	  this command."
1062	- `finalize` accepts T as a runtime override and SC for
1063	  per-side selection. Today's only use site is inside
1064	  `push`'s workflow against a specific repo path, but
1065	  the body itself is generic (squash + commit with
1066	  title/body + push) and works on either side
1067	  standalone — `--scope=code` or `--scope=bot`. Use
1068	  case: a user manually squashing a cycle into one
1069	  commit and pushing. `--squash` is logically optional
1070	  (auto-detect from `@` being empty); `--detach` and
1071	  `--delay` are unnecessary outside `push`'s schedule-
1072	  detached flow but still work.
1073	
1074	### Gap list (refreshed for 0.62.0+)
1075	
1076	The original pre-design gaps are now resolved by the
1077	negotiated decisions (Decisions blocks under each axis).
1078	This list captures the **implementation-cycle gaps** the

# Max review #1 — 0.61.0 por/dual parity design

Captured 2026-05-26 (max effort) after the close-out commit
of 0.61.0 (`c0f3376f`, pre-push to `main`). Independent
review of the design output (audit doc, copying stub,
chores narrative). Each item below carries a **Status** —
open (needs decision), resolved (with the agreed
disposition), or applied (already changed).

## Scope reviewed

- [`por-dual-parity-audit.md`](por-dual-parity-audit.md) —
  canonical design doc post-0.61.0 (≈1090 lines).
- [`copying.md`](copying.md) — copying mechanism design
  stub from `0.61.0-4`.
- [`por-dual-parity.md`](por-dual-parity.md) — older
  forward-looking doc (read for context only; not
  critiqued).
- [`/notes/chores/chores-12.md > ## docs: por/dual parity
  design (0.61.0)`](../chores/chores-12.md#docs-pordual-parity-design-0610) —
  cycle narrative.

Line numbers in this review are **as of the 0.61.0
close-out snapshot**; section / heading names are the
durable references.

## Strengths

For balance — what the bot thinks works well:

- **Three-pass methodology** (audit → commonality
  inversion → axis decomposition). The `-2` inversion is
  the cycle's pivotal move: reframed "we have a problem
  everywhere" to "the right pattern already exists, extend
  it." That mental flip made the rest tractable.
- **Axis decomposition is genuine decomposition.** Calling
  out `--por` as a bundle of six axes opens design space
  the bundle hid. The litmus is `audit.md:752` — "A user
  wanting a fully-plain single repo today can't spell it" —
  a real defect of the bundle framing.
- **A2 collapse was earned.** `.vc-config.toml` write was
  initially listed as independent; the design pass
  noticed presence-of-`.vc-config.toml` ≡ Topology and
  retired the axis. Honest housekeeping.
- **Two-class principle ("defaults where natural; errors
  only for user-specific keys") is sound.** Correct
  generalization of what `resolve_repo` already does.
  Baked-in default config + `vc-x1 config dump` closes
  the "what does layer-4-empty mean?" question
  elegantly.
- **T7-only promotion.** Resisting the urge to seed all
  14 Gaps into Todo is the right restraint — cheapest
  concrete equalization that validates the
  topology-from-config rule as the prototype.
- **Status note + "implementation will diverge" framing**
  (`audit.md:19-37`) is humility the bot will thank
  itself for in 0.65.0.

## Concerns

_All concerns applied and removed from this list; the
Disposition table below and the git log are the record._

## Nits

Lower-priority items; do as time allows.

- **`finalize` matrix row** marks T+SC support based on
  the body being topology-neutral. True, but no command
  path exercises it today. The bot thinks adding a
  footnote — "currently latent — body supports it, no
  caller surfaces it" — would prevent a future cycle
  hardening a use case nobody asked for.

- **Reading guide at top of audit doc.** The doc is ≈1090
  lines and will be cited from many cycles. A `## Reading
  guide` (matrix → §X; one axis → §Y; new axis → §Z)
  costs 10 lines and pays back across many readings. The
  bot thinks this is worth doing before 0.62.0 starts.

- **"Por's view of the chain"** (`audit.md:897-913`)
  duplicates the per-axis Decisions blocks. The bot
  thinks it could be dropped or reframed as a
  debugging cheat-sheet ("when does topology actually
  get consulted?") — a different angle.

- **`validate-todo` / `fix-todo`** are topology-blind by
  design. Worth one sentence somewhere ("notes/-family
  commands operate outside the workspace shape") so a
  future reader doesn't suspect they were missed.

## Process observation

The user-proposed-then-deferred `~/.config/vc-x1/config.
toml` topology default that **opened** the cycle is, by
close-out, **in** the design (`audit.md:763`:
`[default].topology = "single" | "dual"`). The bot's
initial "audit first, expose defaults later" pushback
was about *ordering*, not the idea itself. Net: the cycle
delivered what the user originally proposed, just through
a more thorough process.

The bot thinks this is worth noting because it shows the
pushback shape ("don't ship that *yet*") working
correctly — but also that the original ask was the right
ask. The Decisions blocks currently read as if the design
emerged from the bot's analysis alone; an acknowledgment
of the original user framing would balance the record.

**Status:** open. Optional chores narrative tweak (no
action implied for the design itself).

## Disposition table

| # | Title | Status | Action surface |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| N1 | `finalize` matrix row footnote | Open | `audit.md` matrix |
| N2 | Reading guide at top of audit doc | Open | `audit.md` top |
| N3 | "Por's view of the chain" redundancy | Open | `audit.md` § "Por's view" |
| N4 | `validate-todo` / `fix-todo` topology-blind note | Open | `audit.md` (anywhere appropriate) |
| Pr | Original user framing acknowledgment | Open | Either `chores-12.md` cycle narrative or `audit.md` Decisions blocks |

## TL;DR

The two highest-conviction concerns — 1 (runtime `--por`
semantics) and 3 (copying-surface doubling) — are applied
and removed from this list. What remains (nits N1–N4 and
the process observation) is docs-only: low
risk to defer, but cheaper to apply now in one design pass
than after the next ten cycles cite the current text.

# References

[1]: /notes/design-cli/por-dual-parity-audit.md
[2]: /notes/design-cli/copying.md
[3]: /notes/design-cli/por-dual-parity.md
[4]: /notes/chores/chores-12.md#docs-pordual-parity-design-0610

-  future reader doesn't suspect they were missed.

  the bundle hid. The litmus is `audit.md:752` — "A user
  wanting a fully-plain single repo today can't spell it" —
  a real defect of the bundle framing.
- **A2 collapse was earned.** `.vc-config.toml` write was
  initially listed as independent; the design pass
  noticed presence-of-`.vc-config.toml` ≡ Topology and
  retired the axis. Honest housekeeping.
- **Two-class principle ("defaults where natural; errors
  only for user-specific keys") is sound.** Correct
  generalization of what `resolve_repo` already does.
  Baked-in default config + `vc-x1 config dump` closes
  the "what does layer-4-empty mean?" question
  elegantly.
- **T7-only promotion.** Resisting the urge to seed all
  14 Gaps into Todo is the right restraint — cheapest
  concrete equalization that validates the
  topology-from-config rule as the prototype.
- **Status note + "implementation will diverge" framing**
  (`audit.md:19-37`) is humility the bot will thank
  itself for in 0.65.0.

## Concerns

_All concerns applied and removed from this list; the
Disposition table below and the git log are the record._

## Nits

_All nits (N1–N4) applied and removed from this list; the
Disposition table below and the git log are the record._

## Process observation

The user-proposed-then-deferred `~/.config/vc-x1/config.
toml` topology default that **opened** the cycle is, by
close-out, **in** the design (`audit.md:763`:
`[default].topology = "single" | "dual"`). The bot's
initial "audit first, expose defaults later" pushback
was about *ordering*, not the idea itself. Net: the cycle
delivered what the user originally proposed, just through
a more thorough process.

The bot thinks this is worth noting because it shows the
pushback shape ("don't ship that *yet*") working
correctly — but also that the original ask was the right
ask. The Decisions blocks currently read as if the design
emerged from the bot's analysis alone; an acknowledgment
of the original user framing would balance the record.

**Status:** open. Optional chores narrative tweak (no
action implied for the design itself).

## Disposition table

| # | Title | Status | Action surface |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Pr | Original user framing acknowledgment | Open | Either `chores-12.md` cycle narrative or `audit.md` Decisions blocks |

## TL;DR

The two highest-conviction concerns — 1 (runtime `--por`
semantics) and 3 (copying-surface doubling) — are applied
and removed from this list. What remains (nits N1–N4 and
the process observation) is docs-only: low
risk to defer, but cheaper to apply now in one design pass
than after the next ten cycles cite the current text.

If we're going to run validate-todo (we should probably change to validate-numbering) it needs to check "everything", ideally it should valid sequences in and "#..#" section that has "{0..9}+\. " or some such regx?

content.text:
You're right — `validate-todo` is single-file (it just happened to be pointed at `todo.md`), so calling it "passes" overstates the coverage. For a pre-commit gate that's a real gap: a renumber slip in `bugs.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, or even the `## Ideas` section of `todo.md` (which now has numbered entries — #1, #2) would sail through.

Your design instinct is right, and a few observations sharpen it:

- **Rename**: `validate-todo` → `validate-numbering`, and the `fix-todo` partner → `fix-numbering`. The pair is about numbered-sequence integrity, not todos specifically. Cheap rename (single-user internal tool).
- **It's already *almost* generic.** The "1 Bugs / 45 Todo / 7 Todo" counts show it already detects numbered entries per section by the `^\d+\.␠` anchor and reports by section — the column-0 anchor is what keeps indented sub-lists (3-space) from being miscounted as entries. So the engine is most of the way there; what's todo-specific is mostly the *name* and the single-file scope.
- **"Check everything" = make the multi-file scan the default.** No-arg `validate-numbering` should walk the known notes set (`todo.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, `bugs.md` — and `done.md`/`chores-NN.md` if/when they carry numbered sections), validating *every* `#…#` section that contains `^\d+\.␠` entries, with explicit paths as an override. That's exactly what the pre-commit wants: one invocation, total coverage.
- **Bonus it'd catch for free**: `## Ideas` numbering (now live) and any *new* numbered section someone adds later — no per-section allow-list to maintain.

One thing to decide: scope as "a fixed default file list" vs. "walk `notes/**.md`." I lean fixed-list (predictable, no surprise files), but walking is more future-proof.

Where I'd put this: it's really the substance behind Todo #2's "doc validators" bullet, but it's chunky enough to be its own item. So my proposal:

- **Add a new Todo** — "validate-numbering: rename + all-files + generic section/sequence check" — and have Todo #2 reference it (wiring validators into pre-commit *depends* on this landing).

Two quick calls before I write it:
1. **Fold this capture into `0.62.0-9`** (consistent with how we just folded the Todo #1 reframe and the earlier captures), or keep `-9` as-is and capture separately?
2. **Default scope**: fixed notes-file list, or walk `notes/**.md`?

message.content:
Fold into -9; use a fixed notes-file list and rename fix-todo to fix-numbering as you suggested.
I lean towards defaulting to "notes/**.md" and follow the --init-from convention of single level "@<file-name>" with of list of files/dirs, thoughts?

content.text:
Good — and let me reconcile the two halves of your message, because "use a fixed notes-file list" and "default to `notes/**.md`" pull opposite ways. After thinking about it I'd land on **fixed-list default**, and here's the concrete reason:

**A `notes/**.md` walk would false-positive on prose.** The generic "validate any `#…#` section with `^\d+.␠` entries" rule can't structurally tell a *managed entry sequence* from an *ordinary numbered list*. And our prose docs are full of the latter — `cycle-protocol.md`'s per-commit flow (`1. … 8.`), design notes, etc. Worse, markdown lets writers leave `1. 1. 1.` (it still renders 1-2-3), which is common and legitimate — a walk-everything validator would **block commits** on those. False-positives in a pre-commit gate are worse than a quiet miss, because they erode trust in the gate.

The files where "column-0 number == managed sequence" is actually *true* are exactly the three you named: `todo.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, `bugs.md`. That's a short, stable list — the maintenance cost of "fixed" is near-zero, and it sidesteps the prose problem entirely.

**Your `--init-from` convention is the right call for the *override*, though** — positional files/dirs (a dir → its `*.md`) plus an `@<file>` manifest, additive. That gives you the "point it at anything" flexibility for ad-hoc runs without making the *default* walk everything. Best of both: safe default, flexible override.

So: **fixed-list default + `--init-from`-style override args**, rename the pair (`validate-numbering` / `fix-numbering`), generic per-section detection. Let me capture that as a new Todo and fold it into `-9`. Reading the current Todo section to place it:

   - Adopt one rule, no exception: the pre-commit runs before

   - Enrich the pre-commit so it's meaningful on docs commits:

     exists) to both the documented flow and push's `preflight`

input.new_string:
- Target: its own 0.62.1 cycle (chosen over a 0.61.1 insert
     to avoid rewriting published 0.62.0-x history).
3. **validate-numbering: rename the pair, check all
   sequence-managed notes files generically.** `validate-todo`
   / `fix-todo` only operate on the single file passed, so a
   renumber slip in `bugs.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, or
   `todo.md`'s `## Ideas` section passes unnoticed — too weak
   for a pre-commit gate. Prereq for the pre-commit doc
   validators (Todo "pre-commit: single rule ...").
   - Rename the pair: `validate-todo` → `validate-numbering`,
     `fix-todo` → `fix-numbering` — they validate numbered-
     sequence integrity, not todos specifically.
   - Generic detection: for every `#…#` section, validate the
     column-0 `^\d+\.␠` entries form a contiguous 1..N run.
     Drops the Todo/Bugs special-casing; auto-covers
     `## Ideas` and any new numbered section. Keep the
     column-0 anchor so indented sub-lists aren't counted.
   - Default scope: a fixed list of sequence-managed notes
     files (`todo.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, `bugs.md`) so the
     no-arg pre-commit run covers them all. Fixed rather than
     a `notes/**.md` walk because prose docs
     (`cycle-protocol.md`, design notes) carry ordinary
     numbered lists that aren't managed sequences — a walk
     would false-positive (markdown renders `1. 1. 1.` as
     1-2-3, a legitimate prose pattern).
   - Override args follow the `--init-from` convention:
     positional files/dirs (a dir → its `*.md`) plus an
     `@<file>` manifest, additive — for ad-hoc validation of
     a specific file.
   - Open: revisit fixed-vs-glob at implementation if the

- Target: its own 0.62.1 cycle (chosen over a 0.61.1 insert
     to avoid rewriting published 0.62.0-x history).
3. **validate-numbering: rename the pair, check all
   sequence-managed notes files generically.** `validate-todo`
   / `fix-todo` only operate on the single file passed, so a
   renumber slip in `bugs.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, or
   `todo.md`'s `## Ideas` section passes unnoticed — too weak
   for a pre-commit gate. Prereq for the pre-commit doc
   validators (Todo "pre-commit: single rule ...").
   - Rename the pair: `validate-todo` → `validate-numbering`,
     `fix-todo` → `fix-numbering` — they validate numbered-
     sequence integrity, not todos specifically.
   - Generic detection: for every `#…#` section, validate the
     column-0 `^\d+\.␠` entries form a contiguous 1..N run.
     Drops the Todo/Bugs special-casing; auto-covers
     `## Ideas` and any new numbered section. Keep the
     column-0 anchor so indented sub-lists aren't counted.
   - Default scope: a fixed list of sequence-managed notes
     files (`todo.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, `bugs.md`) so the
     no-arg pre-commit run covers them all. Fixed rather than
     a `notes/**.md` walk because prose docs
     (`cycle-protocol.md`, design notes) carry ordinary
     numbered lists that aren't managed sequences — a walk
     would false-positive (markdown renders `1. 1. 1.` as
     1-2-3, a legitimate prose pattern).
   - Override args follow the `--init-from` convention:
     positional files/dirs (a dir → its `*.md`) plus an
     `@<file>` manifest, additive — for ad-hoc validation of
     a specific file.
   - Open: revisit fixed-vs-glob at implementation if the
     fixed list proves annoying to maintain.
4. **vc-x1 push: support new cycle protocol shape (N:1 code↔bot).**

1	# por/dual parity audit (0.61.0-1)
2	
3	Read-only snapshot audit of every place the `dual` workspace
4	topology (code repo + `.claude/` companion, cross-linked by
5	`ochid:` trailers, configured by `.vc-config.toml`) gets a
6	privileged code path over `por` (Plain Old Repo — single code
7	repo, no `.claude/`, no `.vc-config.toml`). Snapshot taken at
8	commit `463e6bd9` (`docs: prep por/dual parity audit
9	(0.61.0-0)`). Parent design item is T5 in `todo.md` [[1]];
10	design stub is `por-dual-parity.md` [[2]].
11	
12	- Scope: every subcommand, the `--por` flag's reach, the
13	  ochid-touching helpers, test fixtures.
14	- Out of scope: proposing equalization shape (each finding
15	  carries a one-sentence sketch only — concrete fixes are
16	  follow-up cycles).
17	- Method: grep + read-through. No code changes.
18	
19	## Status
20	
21	**Pre-implementation design snapshot.** This document
22	captures the audit findings (`## 1`–`## 8`), the
23	commonality analysis (`## Commonality`), and the
24	negotiated design (`## Feature axes`) at the end of cycle
25	0.61.0. The matrix, the per-axis Decisions blocks, and
26	the resolution chain are the *current target*, not the
27	final shape.
28	
29	**The implementation will diverge from this.** Every
30	design stub does — implementation surfaces edge cases,
31	forces compromises, and reveals constraints the design
32	pass didn't anticipate. Treat what's here as the starting
33	position for 0.62.0+ equalization cycles, not a contract
34	the code must conform to. When implementation finds the
35	design wrong, update the design (or annotate the
36	divergence in chores) — don't bend code to fit a stale
37	sketch.
38	
39	## Reading guide
40	
41	The doc is long (~1200 lines) and cited from many cycles.
42	Jump by task:
43	
44	- **Target surface for a subcommand?** →
45	  [Subcommand × parameter matrix](#subcommand--parameter-matrix).
46	- **How one axis (Topology / Remote / Privacy / Copying)
47	  resolves?** → its Decisions block under
48	  [Feature axes](#feature-axes).
49	- **Where a value comes from (CLI / env / config)?** →
50	  [Resolution chain](#resolution-chain).
51	- **Adding a new axis or flag?** →
52	  [Feature axes](#feature-axes) (axis pattern) + the
53	  matrix Ledger.
54	- **What's still unbuilt?** →
55	  [Gap list](#gap-list-refreshed-for-0620).
56	- **Why today's code diverges?** → the per-subcommand
57	  audit findings (`## 1`–`## 8`) + [Commonality](#commonality).
58	
59	## 1. `options_flags/por.rs` — the `--por` gate
60	
61	- Files touched:
62	  - `src/options_flags/por.rs` (whole file, 1-20).
63	  - Consumers: `src/init.rs:13,61-65,592-596,1199-1203`,
64	    `src/clone.rs:26,48-51,70,81,143-163`,
65	    `src/init/params.rs:39,60`.
66	  - Test references only:
67	    `src/test_helpers.rs:24,105,196`,
68	    `src/init/tests.rs` (many).
69	- Divergence: `PorFlag` is consumed by **exactly two**
70	  subcommands — `init` and `clone`. No other subcommand
71	  surfaces `--por` (`grep "PorFlag" src/` shows zero other
72	  hits). Doc on the flag itself is creation-time framed: "at
73	  creation time" — there is no equivalent runtime flag for
74	  the post-creation commands (`push`, `sync`, `finalize`,
75	  `validate-desc`, `fix-desc`, `show`, etc.) to declare
76	  "treat this workspace as por".
77	- Severity / category: *Defaulting* + *Feature gap*. Absent
78	  flag = dual; `--por` is a creation-only override; runtime
79	  commands infer topology indirectly (presence /
80	  `workspace.other-repo` value of `.vc-config.toml`).
81	- Equalization sketch: The bot thinks the path is to either
82	  promote `PorFlag` into a shared option flag plumbed
83	  through every command, or commit to topology-from-config
84	  uniformly (no `--por` runtime flag, all commands read
85	  `default_scope` / `.vc-config.toml` and branch from
86	  there). The latter is closer to today's `sync`-style
87	  resolution.
88	
89	## 2. `init` — dual privileged at the orchestration layer
90	
91	- Files touched:
92	  - `src/init.rs` — `plan_init` (583-641), `create_por`
93	    (1217-1265), `create_dual` (1280-1380),
94	    `write_por_vc_config` / `write_por_gitignore` (355-363),
95	    `write_code_config` / `write_session_config` (379-391),
96	    dispatch (1199-1203).
97	  - `src/init/params.rs:39,60` — `por: bool` field.
98	- Divergence: Symmetric on the surface (`create_por` /
99	  `create_dual` are sibling orchestrators), but the
100	  defaulting is asymmetric:
101	  - `plan_init` synthesizes `Scope(vec![Side::Code])` when
102	    `params.por` else `Scope(vec![Side::Code, Side::Bot])`
103	    (init.rs:592-596).
104	  - `--config` is rejected unless `--por` is set
105	    (init.rs:608-613, "`--config` is only valid with
106	    `--por` (dual configs are per-side and unconditional)")
107	    — dual gets implicit per-side configs; por gets
108	    user-controllable config and an unconditional
109	    `.gitignore`.
110	  - Dispatch defaults to `create_dual` (init.rs:1199-1203:
111	    `if params.por { create_por } else { create_dual }`).
112	  - The dry-run printout treats dual as the baseline and
113	    emits "(skipped — …)" lines for por
114	    (init.rs:1184-1196).
115	- Severity / category: *Defaulting* (medium — dual is the
116	  default branch; por is the opt-in branch with extra
117	  config-knob asymmetry).
118	- Equalization sketch: The bot thinks the two sibling
119	  orchestrators could share a common config-writing layer
120	  with topology-driven side selection — but the bigger ask
121	  is reframing the dispatch so neither shape is "the
122	  default" at the type level (e.g. a `Topology { Por,
123	  Dual }` enum threaded through, with the default decided by
124	  user config, per the deferred user-config-topology-default
125	  proposal).
126	
127	## 3. `clone` — same shape as init
128	
129	- Files touched:
130	  - `src/clone.rs` — `clone_repo` (113-167), `clone_one`
131	    (180-196), `clone_dual` (202-226), `CloneParams`
132	    (67-85).
133	- Divergence: Mirror of init at the dispatch layer
134	  (clone.rs:156-163: `if params.por { clone_one } else {
135	  clone_dual }`). `clone_one` clones a single repo and
136	  verifies tracking; `clone_dual` clones code + bot
137	  (deriving the bot URL via `derive_session_url`) and
138	  installs the symlink. The bot side is mandatory in
139	  `clone_dual` ("no graceful skip — both sides required by
140	  the default dual shape").

content.text:
Verified across both repos — here's the precise damage:

| Commit | App repo | `.claude` repo |
|---|---|---|
| `-6` | `/.claude/mkorwtpruzqx` ✓ (one, correct) | `/ztwmqmwqpnyw` ✓ (one, correct) |
| `-7` | `/.claude/kroqqqszunur` **×2** (dup) | `/.claude/kroqqqszunur` **(bogus self-ref)** + `/vokukpxyplsw` ✓ |
| `-8` | `/.claude/wnnntkprxxpk` **×2** (dup) | `/.claude/wnnntkprxxpk` **(bogus self-ref)** + `/ovvqwsxrrnvp` ✓ |

So it's clean through `-6` (predates my manual-ochid mistake), and `-7`/`-8` are damaged — the breakage exactly tracks the two pushes where I hand-added `ochid:` to `--body`.

You're right the bot side is worse: the app side is a *harmless duplicate* of the correct value, but the bot side has a **bogus self-referential** `ochid: /.claude/<its-own-chid>` — that's not a bot→app link at all, and it violates the ochid invariant (a bot commit's ochid should point at its app counterpart). The correct bot→app line is also present, so resolution that filters by direction still finds the right target, but the trailer is genuinely wrong.

**Can it be fixed?** Yes, and cleanly — `jj describe` preserves chids, so editing these descriptions to strip the bad lines won't break any cross-link (app→bot and bot→app ochids point at chids that survive the rewrite). Cost is a **force-push of published history** on the affected remote(s), which needs your explicit go-ahead.

Options:

1. **Fix bot `-7`/`-8` only, leave app dups.** The bot self-refs are the real wrong; bot-repo force-push is already routine (finalize does it every push), and protocol explicitly sanctions bot description edits. App dups are harmless redundancy, so we skip rewriting app `main`. *(my lean)*
2. **Fix both repos, `-7` and `-8`.** Fully clean trailers; cost is also rewriting app `main` (heavier, but bounded to 2 commits). Best done now, before `-9` lands on top.
3. **Leave both, document, fix forward.** No rewrite; `-9` onward just omits the manual ochid (already decided). The correct ochid is present in every case.

How do you want to handle it? (And separately — `-9` itself is ready to push with the ochid-free body whenever.)

Honestly? You're right, and my "before" reasoning was weak. Let me give it and then concede:

**Why I said before:** if we repair `-7`/`-8` while `-9` is still uncommitted, then `-9` lands once, on the already-corrected `-8`, and never gets rewritten-after-publishing. Do it after, and rewriting `-7`/`-8` rebases the *already-pushed* `-9` (its git hash changes, though chid is preserved), so the repair force-push touches three commits instead of two.

**Why that's marginal / why after is actually better:** the `-9` rebase is a chid-preserving, content-free description shuffle — harmless, and ochids still resolve. Against that, doing `-9` *first* has two real advantages:
- `-9` lands through the **normal clean `vc-x1 push`** path (fast-forward, no force, standard tooling, ochid auto-stamped right). No entanglement with a remote rewrite.
- The repair becomes a **fully isolated, self-contained step** — one force-push per repo over a known range — instead of interleaving a manual force-push with `vc-x1 push`'s bookmark-tracking / `sync --check` preflight, which would likely complain about the divergence.

So: after-lands wins. Plan:
1. **Push `-9` now** — normal flow, ochid-free body.
2. **Then repair `-7`/`-8`** on both repos (strip the bad trailers; rebases `-9` trivially), force-push both remotes — as its own step with explicit approval.

message.content:
Actually you did the push but then "messaged/talked" to me with what you did.
So there are now 3 new lines in 5c58f42a-7fb7-4ef3-b914-7301ef74ce04.jsonl.
No big deal, two choices do another `vc-x1 finalize ..` or even a `vc-x1 push ..`
but there is no changes to the code repo so I think it's unncessary, redundant
but would do no harm, I hope.

The other is to do nothing leave it and it trully isn't a big deal, thoughts?

content.text:
Do nothing — and not just as "it's small," but because it's the *correct* behavior, not a compromise.

**The tail is inherent and self-resolving.** The `.claude` journal accumulates continuously (CLAUDE.md even says so: "the `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active session"). Those 3 lines aren't orphaned or lost — they sit in the `.claude` working copy and get swept into the **next** `.claude` commit, whenever the next real push happens (`-10`, or the `-7`/`-8` repair). The session data always trails the code commit it talks about by exactly this kind of tail; that's the designed cadence, not a leak.

And it's turtles all the way down: any `finalize`/`push` you run to capture these 3 lines *itself* produces more session text (this exchange, my reply), so you can never drive the `.claude` WC to empty-and-committed while the session is live. Chasing it never converges.

**One correction to "redundant but no harm":**

- **`vc-x1 push` now** is *not* quite harmless — there are no code changes, but push doesn't yet skip `commit-app` on an empty WC (that's exactly the unbuilt half of Todo #3, "skip `commit-app` when code WC is empty"). So today it'd land an **empty code commit** on `main` — minor noise, but real.

message.content:
So you're going to the changes manually not use finalize with new TBs.
I'm guessing we have to do it manually because `vc-x1 finalize` doesn't
"--ignore-immutable" which would be requred for `finalize` to do it, correct?

content.text:
Correct that it's manual, but let me refine the *why* — the `--ignore-immutable` gap is real but it's the secondary reason, not the main one.

**Primary reason — wrong verb/scope.** `vc-x1 finalize` (and `push`) operate on the **working copy** (`@`): "squash/commit the current state, then push." They have no mode that says "go rewrite the body of *that specific past commit* (`-7`, `-8`)." This repair is a surgical history edit of two *named, already-committed* commits — not a finalize of pending work. So finalize is simply the wrong tool here regardless of flags; there's no `finalize -r <-7>` semantics.

**Secondary reason — your point.** Even if finalize *could* target a past commit, `-7`/`-8` are immutable (ancestors of the pushed `main`), so any rewrite needs `--ignore-immutable`, which finalize doesn't expose. But that's downstream of the scope issue.

So the right tool is plain `jj describe -r <chid> -m "<corrected body>" --ignore-immutable` per commit — direct, surgical, no workflow-wrapper in the way. `jj git push --bookmark main` then force-updates each remote (finalize's `--push` is just a thin wrapper over that same `jj git push`, so it'd add nothing).

The dry preview came out right, by the way: each proposed tail ends with a single correctly-directed trailer (app→`/.claude/<bot>`, bot→`/<app>`), and `-9` is already clean (app→`/.claude/ynuxlympoqmt`, bot→`/svskxlppxznp`).

Want me to proceed — apply the four `jj describe` rewrites locally, re-verify, then force-push both `main` remotes?

message.content: Hmm, you say `jj git push --bookmark main` do we need to move main each time from it's current position to where our rework is happening, that feels odd but maybe a --bookmark (branch) is always needed on a push, clarify.

content.text:
Good question — you're conflating two different things, and neither requires moving `main` to the rework. Let me separate them.

**1. We never move `main` to `-7`/`-8`.** The rework happens *below* `main`. `main` is on `-9`, and when I rewrite `-7`/`-8`, jj auto-rebases their descendants (`-8`, `-9`, the empty `@`) — and a bookmark **tracks its commit by chid across rewrites**. So `main`, sitting on `-9` (chid `svskxlppxznp`), automatically follows to the rewritten `-9'` (same chid, new git hash). I don't touch `main` at all; it stays "on `-9`," just on the rebased version of it.

```
before:  -6 ─ -7  ─ -8  ─ -9(main)
rewrite: -6 ─ -7' ─ -8' ─ -9'(main)     ← main rode along, same chid
```

**2. `--bookmark main` on push is a *selector*, not a move.** It tells `jj git push` *which* bookmark to publish to the remote — "push `main` and its ancestors to the remote `main` ref." It does not move the bookmark; it names what to send. You specify it because bare `jj git push` only pushes tracked bookmarks that changed, and being explicit (`--bookmark main`) is the safe, conventional form — it's exactly what `vc-x1 push` does internally. So it's not "always required," but it's the normal explicit way.

The one wrinkle that *is* real: because `-7'`/`-8'`/`-9'` are rewrites (new hashes), pushing `main` is a **non-fast-forward / force update** of the remote ref. jj handles that itself via bookmark tracking — it knows remote `main` = old `-9` and local `main` = new `-9'`, and updates it. No git-style `--force` flag needed; jj just reports "Move sideways bookmark main."

So: no manual `main` moves anywhere — one `jj git push --bookmark main` per repo, each a jj-managed force-update. Want me to go ahead (apply the four `jj describe` rewrites, re-verify, then push both)?

message.content:
It feels funny because I thought jj uses the notion of a "--bookmark" as `main` is a node, where as a "--branch" implies a series of nodes. So I have a mental model that a `jj git push --bookmark main` would push the node at main. For example:
wink@3900x 26-05-30T00:17:53.588Z:~/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1 (main+1)
$ jj log -r @---::main
◆  svskxlpp wink@saville.com 2026-05-29 16:43:24 main a3a1ee29
│  docs: nits N1–N4 + todo captures (0.62.0-9)
◆  ovvqwsxr wink@saville.com 2026-05-29 16:05:05 1d7384c6
│  docs: gap prereq + todo captures (0.62.0-8)
◆  vokukpxy wink@saville.com 2026-05-29 13:38:17 a88108c0
│  docs: list-typed-axis wins rule (0.62.0-7)
~
wink@3900x 26-05-30T00:18:06.109Z:~/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1 (main+1)

content.text:
Your mental model is actually **correct** — and that's exactly why jj renamed "branch" → "bookmark." A bookmark *is* a pointer to a single node (one commit), and the rename was deliberate: unlike a git branch, it doesn't auto-advance when you commit on top. So `--bookmark main` does name the **node** `svskxlpp` (`-9`), not a series. Good instinct.

The thing your model is missing is **git's transfer rule**, which is separate from what a bookmark *is*: pushing a ref sends the node it points at **plus every ancestor object the remote doesn't already have**. You can't put a commit on a remote without its parents — a commit is only valid if its ancestry is present. So:

- `jj git push --bookmark main` = "repoint remote `main` to my local `main`'s node (`-9'`), shipping whatever ancestor objects the remote lacks."
- The remote already has `-6` and below. After our rewrite it's *missing* `-7'`, `-8'`, `-9'` (new hashes). So those three ride along, and remote `main` moves to `-9'`. The old `-7`/`-8`/`-9` objects become unreferenced on the remote (GC'd later).

So the "series" gets transferred not because `--bookmark` means series, but because those three rewritten nodes are new objects required to make the new `main` pointer valid.

And this is the same reason `-9` is "touched" even though we only edit `-7`/`-8`: rewriting `-7` gives it a new hash → `-8'` has a new parent → new hash → `-9'` new hash. The **content and chid of `-9` are unchanged**, but its git hash changes because an ancestor did. Your `@---::main` view is precisely the set that gets new hashes:

```
vokukpxy(-7) ─ ovvqwsxr(-8) ─ svskxlpp(-9, main)
   edit body      edit body       rebased only
   → new hash     → new hash      → new hash, same chid/content
```

In short: one `jj git push --bookmark main` per repo repoints the node, and git carries the three new ancestor objects with it. Shall I proceed?

message.content: One last clarification, how far back do a `jj git push --bookmark main` for new nodes relative to the remote, it would seem more efficient if we proivded the set of nodes we changed i.e. "@---::main" of course if we made a mistake it'd be better to know now. Educate me, please!

content.text:
Great question — this is the heart of git's transfer model, and your efficiency instinct is right; it's just already handled for you.

**How far back: to the nearest common ancestor with the remote — not the root.** On push, the client and server do a quick **negotiation**: the server advertises the commits it already has, the client walks ancestry down from the pushed commit (`-9'`) and stops at the first ancestor the server already has. Here:

```
-9' → -8' → -7' → -6 (STOP — remote already has -6, unchanged hash)
```

So it sends exactly `-7'`, `-8'`, `-9'` — the rewritten frontier. `-6` and everything below are untouched (same hashes), so they're never reconsidered. That frontier *is* effectively your `@---::main` (minus the empty `@`), computed automatically.

**Why you don't hand it the set yourself:**
- **Push is ref-oriented, not range-oriented.** The operation is "make remote `main` = my local `-9'`"; the objects follow by *reachability*. There's no "push these N commits" API — you move a ref, git sends what that requires. So `@---::main` isn't something you'd pass; it's just a *description* of what reachability already selects.
- **Reachability is already minimal and correct**, even if 50 commits had changed — no efficiency left on the table.

**And here it's even cheaper than "3 commits" suggests.** These are *description-only* rewrites — the **trees and blobs are byte-identical** to the old `-7`/`-8`/`-9` (file content unchanged), and the remote already has those objects. So the only *new* objects are the **3 commit objects themselves** (they differ because message + parent-hash changed). Trees/blobs are reused. The actual transfer is three tiny commit headers. Maximally efficient already.

**On "better to know now if we made a mistake":** jj front-runs that — `jj git push` prints the bookmark move (`Move sideways bookmark main from <old> to <new>`) so you see the repoint before trusting it, and the local op-log (`jj op restore`) can undo the rewrite if the *local* result looks wrong. The remote force-update is the one-way door (old commits get orphaned, recoverable only until GC), which is why we previewed the four bodies first. If the remote `main` had drifted (someone else pushed), jj would detect the tracking mismatch and refuse rather than clobber.

Ready to apply the four rewrites, re-verify locally, and push — or do you want to eyeball anything else first?

message.content: Sorry, another Q: if this was linux that "list" you mention sending could be huge 1,000's commits from some point to the root, I assume git negotiaties it smarter than here is the list I have, or I probably miss-understood.

content.text:
You understood correctly — and your suspicion is right: git **never** ships "tip-to-root, here's everything I have." It computes the **divergence**, bounded by how far your branch has drifted from the remote, not by total history depth. Two different mechanisms depending on direction:

**Push (our case) — reachability cut, no per-commit chatter.** The receiving side advertises only its **ref tips** (bounded by number of branches/tags — thousands at most, not millions of commits). The sending client then computes, *locally*, the set:

> objects reachable from `<commit I'm pushing>` **but not** reachable from any remote tip

i.e. `git pack-objects` with the remote tips as *negative* refs (`-9' --not <remote main, all other remote tips>`). It paints everything reachable from the remote tips "uninteresting" and sends only what's left. The crucial scale trick: once a commit is painted uninteresting, **all its ancestors are too**, so the walk **stops at the shared frontier** — it never descends into the millions of common ancestors. With the **commit-graph + generation numbers**, git prunes that walk hard. So the work is O(your divergence), not O(history). In Linux, pushing 3 new commits onto an up-to-date remote computes the same tiny delta we have here.

**Fetch (the symmetric case)** is where the real *negotiation* happens, because neither side knows the other's full state. The client sends `want <hash>` then `have <hash>` lines walking back from its refs — but in **exponentially-stepping batches** (skip 1, 2, 4, 8… commits), so the server ACKs a common base in a handful of round-trips — logarithmic-ish, not linear. Again bounded by divergence.

So the "list" is always the **delta to the nearest common frontier**, found by reachability (push) or a few negotiation rounds (fetch) — never an enumeration to the root.

Our situation is just the degenerate small instance: remote and local share everything through `-6` (untouched hashes), divergence is `-7'`/`-8'`/`-9'`, so the cut yields three commit objects (and even their trees/blobs are reused). Same algorithm Linux uses; tiny inputs.

Ready to apply the four rewrites and push whenever you give the word.

_All nits (N1–N4) applied and removed from this list; the
Disposition table below and the git log are the record._

## Process observation

The user-proposed-then-deferred `~/.config/vc-x1/config.
toml` topology default that **opened** the cycle is, by
close-out, **in** the design (`audit.md:763`:
`[default].topology = "single" | "dual"`). The bot's
initial "audit first, expose defaults later" pushback
was about *ordering*, not the idea itself. Net: the cycle
delivered what the user originally proposed, just through
a more thorough process.

The bot thinks this is worth noting because it shows the
pushback shape ("don't ship that *yet*") working
correctly — but also that the original ask was the right
ask. The Decisions blocks currently read as if the design
emerged from the bot's analysis alone; an acknowledgment
of the original user framing would balance the record.

**Status:** open. Optional chores narrative tweak (no
action implied for the design itself).
